main_summary.summary: Gloria Claudette Grinnell, interviewed April 14, 2013 in Ohio by David Cline for the Southern Oral History Program in partnership with the Library of Congress and the Smithsonian National Museum of African American History and Culture, recounts her path from a San Francisco and San Diego childhood to participation in the February 1960 Richmond sit-ins as a Virginia Union University student. Her great-great-grandparent helped found Virginia Union, where she enrolled after a West Coast upbringing in the Western Addition and the Presidio that included isolating experiences at St. Dominic's Catholic school. She joined the 34-student Virginia Union sit-in led by Charles Sherrod at Thalhimer's department store in downtown Richmond, was arrested, taken to the Paddy wagon, and held in a segregated courtroom. Her uncle, the Norfolk NAACP attorney J. Hugo Madison, connected her to attorney Martin A. Martin for legal defense. She graduated in 1962, completed a master's at USC and a doctorate in human behavior, taught graduate career psychology at California Lutheran University, and worked as an expulsion counselor for the Los Angeles Unified School District under California Assembly Bill 922. She reflects on Adam Clayton Powell, the March on Washington, the Obama era, and the 2013 Shelby County voting-rights challenge.
key_themes: West Coast Black childhood and migration
main_summary.historical_significance:
Grinnell's testimony documents the Virginia Union University sit-in cohort, one of the earliest wave of student sit-ins (three weeks after Greensboro, February 1960), from the perspective of a participant who arrived from California and experienced Southern segregation as an outsider. Her account names Charles Sherrod as a Virginia Union sit-in leader before his SNCC work in Albany, situates the legal defense within the Virginia NAACP infrastructure (her uncle J. Hugo Madison; attorney Martin A. Martin), and preserves the Norfolk Greyhound bus integration her aunt undertook in coordination with Adam Clayton Powell.

The interview also documents the long-tail employment consequences of a 1960 sit-in arrest record: ten attempts to pass the LAUSD training-and-experience screening, a District of Columbia hiring officer who openly cited her arrest record. Her later expulsion-counselor work at LAUSD provides primary-source observation of racially disparate discipline outcomes for inner-city students versus white students with college trajectories, and her LAUSD textbook-committee account of token Black-history images placed outside chapter content offers a specific instance of curricular tokenism in the post-civil-rights era.

chapters.title: Grandparents and Family Migration
chapters.summary: Grinnell traces her grandparents' move from Virginia to Philadelphia and the family split that followed when Philadelphia would not hire Black teachers. Her grandmother took in laundry while her grandfather refused that work; the older children went to college and became teachers and helped pay for the youngest's education. A great-great-grandparent was among the founders of Virginia Union University.

chapters.title: San Francisco Childhood
chapters.summary: After her parents divorced, Grinnell's mother took her to San Francisco, settling first in the Western Addition and then the Presidio. At St. Dominic's on Pine and Steiner she was the only Black child; a nun slapped her during morning prayers and marked her papers with failing grades until her mother confronted the school. At a public school called Presidio a white boy told her his mother forbade him to hold the hand of a 'nigger'-her first encounter with the slur.

chapters.title: Bay Area Black Adult Memories
chapters.summary: From the family mom-and-pop grocery store Grinnell overheard her parents discussing a Black teacher reduced to substitute work because of a dishonorable World War II discharge after he jumped ship rather than fight for a country that would not free him. She remembers her mother caught in the union-job catch-22, told to join the union first to get a teaching job, told to get the job first by the union. She also recalls the unmarked rope across Santa Monica's beach and an awkward Filipino picnic where a gatekeeper questioned her presence.

chapters.title: Virginia Union and Southern Racial Awakening
chapters.summary: Grinnell arrived at Virginia Union University in the late 1950s. The first thing she did in Richmond was sit at a soda fountain and order a banana split; the lady went to make it then returned to say she could not be served. She describes her initial anger at Black students who would not sit in, before later understanding that their parents would lose jobs. Her favorite instructor was Dr. Bob Johnson, who held two PhDs.

chapters.title: Thalhimer's Sit-in and Arrest
chapters.summary: Grinnell joined Charles Sherrod's student organizing meetings at Virginia Union. The students walked downtown to Thalhimer's department store. Standing rather than sitting because of her height plus heels, she was visible to police; the sit-in group of 34 was arrested and loaded into a Paddy wagon. The jail cell was smelly, the courtroom was segregated, and she watched a drunken Black man brought in and realized the courts themselves operated by color line. Her aunt called to say she had seen her on the news being helped into the Paddy wagon.

chapters.title: Adam Clayton Powell and Nonviolence
chapters.summary: Grinnell describes attending an Adam Clayton Powell civil-rights meeting in a Richmond-area church and being startled by the congregational clapping and foot-stomping, she had never been in a large gathering of Black people. Adam Clayton Powell had worked with her uncle. She committed to nonviolence, reasoning that protected response was not really fair but was the position she would hold. She acknowledges she might have responded differently if she had been a man.

chapters.title: Return West and the Arrest Record
chapters.summary: Grinnell graduated from Virginia Union in 1962 and moved through DC and San Francisco before settling in Los Angeles. A DC hiring officer told her he had to hire her because of her test score but that her arrest record from the sit-in meant she would not be happy there. She passed the Los Angeles Unified School District written exam ten times before being admitted; LAUSD failed her on training and experience because of the sit-in arrest record. She eventually got in when someone mistakenly sent her a contract.

chapters.title: Obama Era and Henry Kissinger
chapters.summary: Grinnell describes the surfacing of negative racial sentiment during the Obama presidency, the shouts during congressional sessions, the Hitler comparisons. Her brother, who worked as part of the staff supporting Henry Kissinger in Switzerland, came back insisting that the racial framing in U.S. politics was beside the point compared to actual world problems. She names herself elated at Obama's election not because he was Black but because he was intelligent and would work for everyone.

chapters.title: LAUSD Expulsion Counseling and Busing
chapters.summary: As an expulsion counselor Grinnell sat on three-person committees reviewing students brought up on weapons or drug violations. She describes one case of a white student with affluent parents who was guilty of drug involvement but not expelled because, the other committee members argued, he would go to college. She wrote a three-page letter protesting the disparate outcomes. She also describes the LAUSD busing program that moved Black children out of high-achieving Black-neighborhood schools like the one near her View Park home and notes parents' resistance.

chapters.title: Reflections from Ohio
chapters.summary: Grinnell, now living in a small Ohio town for nine years, observes that change has come but in coded form: covert rather than overt racism, a Black child's locker labeled with a slur in a nearby Ohio high school not long before she moved there. She kept her Los Angeles house in case a cross was burned on the Ohio lawn. She describes the town, boutiques, a theater favored by local directors, walking trails, helicopters that are private rather than the LA 'ghetto birds' she remembers from working the projects.

chapters.title: Family Legacy and Norfolk Greyhound Integration
chapters.summary: Grinnell's uncle, J. Hugo Madison, the Virginia NAACP attorney who handled Norfolk-region legal work, called her 'screwball' and connected her to attorney Martin A. Martin for the sit-in defense. Her aunt, traveling from Virginia to DC by Greyhound bus with Grinnell's mother and another sister, deliberately sat in the white section and was arrested to test the post-Morgan v. Virginia integration ruling that Virginia was not enforcing. She coordinated the arrest with Hugo Madison and Adam Clayton Powell. The interview is conducted during the Supreme Court's Shelby County voting-rights challenge.
related_events: Shelby County v. Holder oral arguments February 2013

chapters.title: Green Book Era and Integration's Costs
chapters.summary: Grinnell remembers traveling with her parents who would stop at Black homes asking where Black travelers could stay, learning of Mrs. Jones's spare room. She recalls The Negro Motorist Green Book, the AAA-equivalent guide that listed where Black motorists could stay. She names Peg Leg Bates's upstate New York resort as an example of Black-owned hospitality businesses that closed when integration arrived. She wrote letters to the Los Angeles Times and local papers protesting the lowercasing of 'black' in print, arguing that 'white' is generic but 'black' designates a specific American population.

loc_video.caption: Gloria Claudette Grinnell oral history interview conducted by David P. Cline in Ojai, California, 2013 April 14
